title: Image formation by a thin lens: why the ray diagram is the lens equation
scenario: A converging lens with focal length $f = 1$ m. An object $h_o = 0.4$ m tall stands a distance $d_o$ to the left. Slide the object along the axis: outside the focal point it makes a **real, inverted** image you could catch on a screen (a projector); slide it *inside* the focal point and the image flips to **virtual, upright, and enlarged** (a magnifying glass). The thin-lens equation $1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f$@{optics-thin-lens} tells you *where* the image is — but the **ray diagram** shows you *why*, with three straight lines and no equation at all.

assumptions.claim: A *thin* lens: its thickness is negligible next to the focal length, so both refractions happen in one plane at the lens center.
*Paraxial* rays — all rays stay close to the axis, so a point object maps to a single point image. Real lenses with wide apertures blur this (spherical aberration).
A single converging lens, $f > 0$, with the sign convention that a real image (on the far side, catchable on a screen) has $d_i > 0$ and a virtual image has $d_i < 0$.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two rules)
steps.prose: Two memorized equations. The first locates the image; the second gives its size and orientation. A negative $m$ means the image is inverted; $|m| < 1$ means reduced; a negative $d_i$ means the image is virtual (on the object's side).

steps.label: Solve the lens equation for the image distance
steps.prose: With $f = 1$ m and $d_o = 1.5$ m, the image distance is $d_i = 3$ m. It is **positive**, so the image is **real** — it forms on the far side of the lens, where you could place a screen.

steps.label: Read off the magnification and image height
steps.prose: The magnification is $m = -2$: the sign is negative, so the image is **inverted**, and $|m| > 1$, so it is **enlarged** — this is the projector regime ($f < d_o < 2f$). Slide the object *inside* the focal point and $m$ flips positive — a magnifying glass.

image_distance.label: Image distance $d_i = d_o f/(d_o-f)$ at $d_o = 1.5$ m

image_height.label: Image height $h_i = m\,h_o$ at $d_o = 1.5$ m, $h_o = 0.4$ m

steps.label: Draw three rays from the tip of the object — no equation needed
steps.prose: Geometric optics needs no calculus and, at first, no algebra. From the object tip, the ray *parallel* to the axis bends through the far focus $F'$; the ray through the lens *center* goes straight; the ray through the near focus $F$ leaves *parallel*. Each is a straight line you can rule by hand. Where they cross is the image.

steps.label: Two of the rays give the magnification two different ways (similar triangles)
steps.prose: The center ray makes two similar triangles (object–axis–center and image–axis–center), so $h_i/h_o = -d_i/d_o$. The parallel ray makes two more about a focal point, so $h_i/h_o = -(d_i - f)/f$. Same ratio $h_i/h_o$, computed from two different rays.

steps.label: Set the two equal — and the thin-lens equation falls out
steps.prose: Demanding that the two rays cross at one point — that the two magnifications agree — and clearing fractions gives $1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i$ exactly. The equation you memorized is the algebra of *the rays meet*. (Deeper still, Fermat's principle: every path takes a stationary time, $dL = 0$ — the one genuinely calculus statement underneath it all.)

proof.heading: The thin-lens equation is the algebra of “the three principal-ray constructions locate one image” — SymPy proves the chief, parallel, and focal rays all agree.
proof.detail: check $d_i = d_o f/(d_o-f)$ solves $1/d_o+1/d_i=1/f$; chief ray $m=-d_i/d_o$; parallel & focal rays give the same $m$; $m = f/(f-d_o)$

checks.claim: The closed form $d_i = \dfrac{d_o f}{d_o - f}$ satisfies the thin-lens equation $\dfrac{1}{d_o} + \dfrac{1}{d_i} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ — back-substituting leaves zero.
The ray through the lens *center* is undeviated, so the object and image triangles about the center are similar: $m = h_i/h_o = -d_i/d_o$.

The *parallel* ray refracts toward (or away from) the focus, giving $m = -(d_i - f)/f$ by similar triangles. It equals the chief-ray value $-d_i/d_o$ — the two independent constructions locate the *same* image point, and that agreement is exactly the thin-lens equation.
The *focal* ray (through a focus, emerging parallel) gives $m = -f/(d_o - f)$ — the same magnification again. All three principal-ray constructions locate one image point.
The magnification in terms of $d_o, f$ alone: $m = -\dfrac{d_i}{d_o} = \dfrac{f}{f - d_o}$ — its sign sets the orientation (upright when $m>0$, inverted when $m<0$), its size the scale.

misconception.claim: A taller object is magnified more — the magnification depends on the object's size.
misconception.refuted_by: It does not. The magnification $m = -d_i/d_o$ is fixed by the two *distances* alone — a property of the lens and where you place the object, not of the object's height. Drag the object-height slider: the image grows and shrinks in step with the object, but $m$ (and whether the image is enlarged or reduced) never changes. Magnification is about the *geometry of the rays*, which is why the same lens magnifies a tall tree and a short flower by the same factor at the same distance.

practice.prompt: A converging lens has focal length $f = 1$ m. An object sits at $d_o = 1.5$ m. Where does the image form — what is $d_i$?

choices.misconception: Subtracted the distances ($d_o - f$). The *reciprocals* add: $1/d_i = 1/f - 1/d_o$, so $d_i = d_o f/(d_o - f)$ — you cannot just subtract $f$ from $d_o$.
Used $d_o + f$ in the denominator. Solving $1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f$ gives $d_i = d_o f/(d_o - f)$, with a *minus* sign — the two terms are subtracted when you isolate $1/d_i$.
The image forms *at* the focal point only for an object infinitely far away. Here $d_o$ is finite, so the image is farther out: $d_i = d_o f/(d_o - f)$.

From the object tip, the ray *parallel* to the axis bends through the far focus $F'$; the ray through the lens *center* goes straight; the ray through the near focus $F$ leaves *parallel*. Each is a straight line you can rule by hand. Where they cross is the image.

calculus_steps.prose: The center ray makes two similar triangles (object–axis–center and image–axis–center), so $h_i/h_o = -d_i/d_o$. The parallel ray makes two more about a focal point, so $h_i/h_o = -(d_i - f)/f$. Same ratio $h_i/h_o$, computed from two different rays.

calculus_steps.prose: Demanding that the two rays cross at one point — that the two magnifications agree — and clearing fractions gives $1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i$ exactly. practice.prompt: The same lens ($f = 1$ m) with the object at $d_o = 1.5$ m forms its image at $d_i = 3$ m. What is the magnification $m$?

choices.misconception: Dropped the minus sign. A real image is *inverted*, so $m = -d_i/d_o$ is negative. A positive $m$ would mean an upright image — which only happens for a virtual image, $d_o < f$.
Flipped the ratio to $-d_o/d_i$. Magnification is image distance *over* object distance: $m = -d_i/d_o$, not $-d_o/d_i$.

algebra_steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two rules)
